The earliest known glass artifacts date back to around 3600 BCE in Mesopotamia.
Ancient Egyptians started producing glass as early as 1500 BCE for decorative purposes.
Roman Empire advanced glassmaking techniques, developing window glass and vessels using blowpipes.
Venice became a renowned center for high-quality glass production during the Middle Ages (Murano glass).
19th-century innovations included soda-lime glass and borosilicate glass (Pyrex).
Common glass is made of silica, sodium oxide, and calcium oxide.
Glass is amorphous with no long-range order in its atomic structure.
Glass exhibits transparency due to the absence of grain boundaries that scatter light.
Glass has a high melting point but can shatter under thermal stress.
Glass is brittle and breaks without significant deformation.
The refractive index of glass makes it useful for lenses and optical fibers.
Glass is an insulator, ideal for electrical applications.
